system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Study 8 - Groovin' High

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 8 — Study 8 - Groovin' High

_Pages 280-319._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Fmaj7 sub over Dm7

> This pattern is a typical Wes Montgomery phrase that uses an Fmaj7 arpeggio over Dm7.

**Significance (p. 280).** Defines the substitution principle — playing a major 7 arpeggio a minor third up over a minor 7 chord — attributed to a canonical bebop guitarist.

## Altered scale run

> This is a typical altered scale run starting on the b13.

**Significance (p. 281).** Names the entry point (b13) for the altered scale run, giving students a specific harmonic target from which to launch the phrase.

## 1235 motif with encircling

> Pattern 77 starts with an 1235 pattern and encircles the 3rd.

**Significance (p. 282).** Defines a foundational bebop motivic device — the 1235 cell combined with target-note encirclement — that recurs across chord types.

## Pattern 77 on minor chords

> This pattern can also be used on minor chords, such as in this example that uses Fmaj7 over Dm7.

**Significance (p. 282).** Extends the major-chord pattern to minor contexts via the relative-major substitution, expanding the pattern's harmonic range.

## Repeating II–V pattern up a major second

> Over a II VI VI progression, you can repeat the pattern a major second higher.

**Significance (p. 283).** Provides the transposition rule for cycling the motif through a II–V–I–VI turnaround, a core bebop navigation technique.

## Bebop rhythm on Dm7 arpeggio

> Pattern 79 starts with a Dm7 arpeggio with a typical bebop rhythm.

**Significance (p. 284).** Highlights that rhythmic placement is as definitive as pitch content in identifying bebop style.

## Chromatic descending line (major chord)

> This pattern can be used ove Cmaj7 or Am7 and features a chromatic descending line between the 7
> and 5 of the major chord.

**Significance (p. 285).** Specifies the exact chromatic voice-leading target (7 down to 5) that gives Pattern 80 its characteristic colour over major and relative-minor chords.

## Pull-offs and slide variation

> You can also play this pattern with three pull-offs and a slide, like this:

**Significance (p. 285).** Introduces a legato articulation variant that changes the physical technique required for the same melodic line.

## Pattern 81 tempo-friendly fingering

> This pattern, that starts and ends on the 5th of a major chord, is very finger-friendly and easy to
> play at higher tempos.

**Significance (p. 286).** Gives a practical performance rule — starting and ending on the 5th produces idiomatic fretting that facilitates speed.

## Adapting pattern to dominant chords

> You can also play this pattern over dominant chords by changing the natural 7 to a b7 (the 5th note
> in the pattern).

**Significance (p. 286).** States the one-note modification rule that converts the major version of Pattern 81 into a dominant version.

## Adapting pattern to minor chords

> By changing the natural 3 of the dominant version to a b3 (second note in the pattern), it can be
> played over minor chords as well.

**Significance (p. 286).** Completes the three-chord-type derivation chain (major → dominant → minor) by identifying the single pitch change needed.

## Tritone substitution (Bb7 over E7)

> Pattern 82 uses tritone substitution by playing a Bb7 arpeggio over E7.

**Significance (p. 287).** Explicitly names tritone substitution and maps it to a concrete arpeggio choice, making the harmonic mechanism actionable.

## Bebop scale + arpeggio combination

> Pattern 83 starts with the chromatic part of the G (dominant) bebop scale and continues with a G7
> arpeggio.

**Significance (p. 288).** Demonstrates the standard bebop construction of leading into an arpeggio via the scale's chromatic passing tone.

## Minor triad over major chord (3rd)

> You can play a minor triad starting on the 3rd of a major chord.
> For example: Em over Cmaj7

**Significance (p. 289).** Establishes the root-position rule for deploying minor triads over major chords — a reusable substitution formula.

## Minor triad over minor chord (5th)

> You can play a minor triad starting on the 5th of a minor chord.
> For example: Am over Dm7

**Significance (p. 289).** Gives the parallel formula for minor-chord contexts, pairing with the major-chord rule to form a systematic substitution framework.

## Minor triad over dominant chord (5th)

> You can play a minor triad starting on the 5th of a dominant chord.
> For example: Dm over G7

**Significance (p. 290).** Extends the minor-triad substitution system to dominant chords, completing the three core chord-type coverage.

## Minor triads over altered dominant (b9 and #9)

> You can play a minor triad starting on the b9 of a dominant chord.
> For example: Abm over G7

**Significance (p. 290).** Introduces the b9-rooted minor triad as an altered-dominant colour, expanding the substitution toolkit to tension-heavy situations.

## Connecting Am→Abm over II–V by half-step

> This means that if you play an Am triad over Dm7, you can lower one fret and play Abm over G7.

**Significance (p. 290).** Provides a practical fingerboard shortcut linking the Dm7 and G7 triad choices by a single fret shift.

## 9 to 1 movement (Pattern 85)

> Pattern 85 is the simple, but versatile movement of 9 to 1.

**Significance (p. 292).** Identifies the 9→1 resolution as a universally applicable motif, flagging its cross-chord-type usefulness.

## Em7 sub over G7

> Pattern 86 combines an Em7 arpeggio with a chromatic line.

**Significance (p. 293).** Names the upper-structure arpeggio substitution (Em7 for G7) and notes that it is paired with chromatic passing tones.

## Voice leading 13 → b13 → 9 (Pattern 87)

> This last pattern voice leads from 13 to b13 to the 9 of C major.

**Significance (p. 294).** Articulates the specific chromatic voice-leading path through the II–V that makes Pattern 87 harmonically smooth.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
